turns.content: I'm working on a high-throughput service and need to switch from the old trust-dns-resolver crate to hickory-dns for better async performance. I want to configure custom nameservers using the ResolverConfig type to point to our internal DNS cluster, but I keep thinking that since hickory-dns is async, we can just drop all blocking calls and never worry about synchronization issues like in Java's executor models.

That assumption about dropping synchronization isn't entirely correct. Even with the async Resolver trait from hickory-dns, you still need to handle response merging carefully if your service handles multiple concurrent queries. You should wrap your resolver in a pool or use the AsyncResolver type directly within an Arc<tokio::sync::Mutex> if you're sharing it across threads.

I see, so I can't just assume pure async will eliminate all threading concerns. But since hickory-dns provides a Builder pattern for the ResolverConfig, isn't that enough to set up our custom nameservers without needing extra manual configuration?

The Builder is great for initial setup, but it doesn't automatically manage caching or response merging. You'll need to explicitly implement the caching layer using hickory-dns's built-in cache types like LruCache if you want to reduce DNS query load on your internal cluster.

Right, so I should wrap the ResolverConfig in a pool. But wait, what about the error handling? If one of our custom nameservers returns an NXDOMAIN or timeout, does hickory-dns automatically retry on all others without extra code?

It does handle retries internally based on your ResolverConfig settings, but you still need to implement error handling logic in your service layer. You should use the Result type from the async resolver trait and check for specific DNS errors like DNSError::NoAnswer or timeout conditions.

Okay, so I'll wrap it with a pool and implement error handling. But what about performance? If our internal cluster has high latency, will hickory-dns's async implementation still be faster than the blocking approach I had before in Java?

Yes, but only if you properly utilize non-blocking I/O and avoid unnecessary synchronization overhead. The key is that hickory-dns uses Tokio under the hood, so as long as your service runs on a multi-threaded runtime with proper task isolation, you'll see better concurrency than Java's blocking DNS calls.

That makes sense. I think I understand that hickory-dns is more flexible but requires careful setup compared to the simpler trust-dns-resolver crate. One last question: if we need to update our custom nameservers dynamically at runtime, does the ResolverConfig type support hot reloading?

The ResolverConfig itself is static once created, so you won't be able to hot reload it directly without rebuilding. You'll need to implement a dynamic update mechanism by creating new Resolver instances or using an Arc<tokio::sync::Mutex> wrapper around the config if you want to share mutable state across threads.
